segments.s172-g145: که begins a subordinate/cause clause here meaning “because.” بیزار گشتیم is a compound verb phrase: بیزار (averse) + گشتیم (past plural of شدن, “we became”), so “we became averse.” ز is the poetic preposition “of/from” linking the feeling to its object (افراسیاب). The whole segment = “because we became averse of Afrasiab.”
segments.s172-g146: نخواهیم is the negative of خواهیم used here with a future/intent sense (“we will not”); دیدار is the noun ‘meeting/seeing.’ او را uses او as the third‑person pronoun and را as the definite/object/topic marker placed after the pronoun. به خواب uses به + خواب to mean “in a dream/in sleep.” The clause = “we will not see him in (a) dream.”
segments.s172-g147: ازان is a demonstrative‑prepositional phrase “from that/ because of that.” خون is the noun “blood.” که introduces a relative clause “which.” او ریخت uses او (he) + ریخت (here the verbal sense “poured/ shed/ spilled”), and بر بیگناه = بر + بیگناه (“upon/against the innocent”). The whole segment = “from that blood which he shed upon the innocent.”
segments.s172-g148: کسی را نبود is a negative existential in past tense: کسی را (emphatic/topic marking after کسی) + نبود (“there was not / there was no one”). اندر آن روی راه is a locative phrase: اندر = “in/on,” آن روی = “that side/face,” راه = “road/way.” The phrase means “there was no one on that side of the road.”
segments.s172-g149: کنون is the temporal adverb “now.” انجمن‌گر here functions as a nominal/epithet referring to the (former) gathered company or the notion of an assembly; پراگنده‌ایم is the present plural copula with past‑result sense “we are scattered” (پراگنده + -ایم). Together: “Now (the) assembly is scattered” / “we, a gathered company, are scattered.”
segments.s172-g150: همه پیش تو places the subject: همه (“all”) + پیش تو (“before you / in your presence”). چاکر و بنده‌ایم is the predicate: two nouns linked by و and the copular ending -ایم (“we are servants and slaves”). Word order is subject/adverbial first, then predicate: “All of us before you are your servants and slaves.”
segments.s172-g151: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning “when.” چیره شدی is the verbal phrase “you became dominant/overcame/you prevail(ed)” (چیره = dominant/prevailing; شدی = past/perfect of شدن “you became”). بیگنه خون مریز is an imperative negative construction with مریز (“do not spill”) applied to خون (“blood”) qualified by بیگنه (“of strangers/foreign/alien”); the whole = “When you have prevailed, do not spill the blood of strangers.”
segments.s172-g152: مکن is a negative imperative particle “do not.” The following noun phrase is the object/what not to do: چنگ (“claw”) + گردون (here used as a genitival/epithet meaning ‘of the heavens/fate’) + گردنده (“turning/whirling” used attributively) + تیز (“sharp/keen”). Grammatically, the modifiers follow the head in this poetic order: “do not (set loose) the heavens’ sharp, whirling claw.”
segments.s172-g113: رسیدند is a past‑tense, third‑person plural verb meaning 'they arrived.' The subject (یاران لشکر) follows it in the line as a topical/poetic order: یاران (companions/followers) + لشکر (of the army). بدوی appears as a descriptive/qualifying word after the noun phrase; in classical poetry adjectives or adverbs can follow the noun they modify.
segments.s172-g114: غمی is a noun in the accusative/genitive sense 'sorrow, grief' describing the state in which they found him. یافتندش is past tense, 3rd‑person plural + enclitic object -ش 'him' (they found him). پر از is the idiom 'full of' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of/from'). آب روی is a compact poetic phrase where آب (water/tears) qualifies روی (face) — 'water on his face' i.e. tears.
segments.s172-g115: گرفتند is past tense, 3rd‑person plural 'they took/ seized' used here idiomatically with 'نفرین' to mean 'they uttered/took a curse.' نفرین is the direct object 'a curse.' بران is the prepositional/relative particle 'upon that' (بران = بر + آن in older/poetic form) pointing to 'that [person].' رهنمای is the noun 'guide' (object of the curse).
segments.s172-g116: به زخمش uses به + noun to mark direction/target 'toward his wound' (زخمش = زخم + ـش 'his wound'). فگندند is an older past plural of فکندند (they threw/cast); here it is used reflexively/figuratively—'cast themselves' or 'threw themselves down.' هر یک ز پای is a poetic expression 'each one from [their] feet' i.e. each one fell down (to his feet/knees); هر یک = 'each one', ز = poetic 'from', پای = 'foot/feet.'
segments.s172-g117: زواره functions as a proper name here (the context gives it as a person who then swears). یکی is 'one' or 'a certain one' (introducing a single person). سخت modifies سوگند and here is adverbial: 'firmly' or 'solemnly.' سوگند خورد is a fixed idiom 'to take/utter an oath' where خورد is an auxiliary-like verb; سوگند is the noun 'oath' and خورد completes the phrase in past tense 'swore.'
segments.s172-g118: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'poured down' or 'fell down' (فرو 'downward' + ریخت 'poured/flowed'). از دیدگان marks source/point of origin 'from the eyes.' آب زرد is a poetic collocation 'yellow/pale water' used for 'tears'; the adjective زرد follows the noun it qualifies.
segments.s172-g119: کزین پس is a temporal phrase 'from this on / henceforth' (کزین = 'from this', پس = 'after/then'). نه ... جویم is negative coordination: نه نخچیر جویم = 'I will not seek spoils/game' (جویم is first‑person present/stative 'I seek' used here with a future/habitual sense). نه خواب adds the second coordinated negative 'nor sleep' (i.e. I will neither seek spoils nor sleep). Word order places the negation particle نه before each coordinated object.
segments.s172-g120: نپردازم is a negative present/future 1st‑person singular form meaning 'I will not rest / I will not sleep/let sleep come' (نـ prefix + پردازم). از کین marks cause 'because of/from the hatred/vengeance' (کین = 'vengeance, hatred'), and افراسیاب is the proper name 'Afrasiab' as the source of that enmity. Together the clause says he will not rest because of Afrasiab's hatred.
segments.s172-g65: ستودش is a past-tense verb (ستود + -ش object suffix) meaning 'praised him/it'. فراوان is an adverb modifying the verb ('greatly'/'profusely'). و کرد آفرین is a coordinated clause: کرد is the simple past 'did/made' used with the noun/interjection آفرین ('praise'/'bravo'), literally 'and made/uttered praise' — a common poetic idiom meaning 'and cried "bravo"/offered praise'.
segments.s172-g66: که introduces a subordinate explanatory clause ('for/that'). چون تو کسی نیست is an idiomatic negated existential: 'there is no one like you' — چون = 'like/as', کسی نیست = 'no one exists'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', and ایران‌زمین = 'the Iranian land/realm'. Word order places the simile 'چون تو' before 'کسی نیست' for emphasis.
segments.s172-g67: A paratactic list of abstract qualities: بزرگی (greatness), فر (the special royal glory/farr), بلندی (loftiness) and داد (here the noun 'justice'). The repeated و simply links the nouns; داد is a noun, not the past tense of 'to give' in this line. Note: the provided sense list for بلندی does not match the poetic meaning 'loftiness', so a literal sense index is not available.
segments.s172-g68: همان here means 'that very' / 'those same' qualifying بزم و رزم ('feast(s) and battle(s)'). از تو داریم یاد is literally 'we have remembrance of you' — از + noun = 'of/from you', داریم = present 'we have', and یاد = 'remembrance/memory'. The clause states that those feasts and fights are remembered on account of you.
segments.s172-g69: ترا is the object/topic form of 'you' (here functioning as the sentence's topic: 'you, ...'). با هنر is an instrumental phrase 'with/by skill'. گوهرست is an archaic copular expression 'is a gem' (گوهر + است fused), and خرد ('wisdom') is coordinated with گوهر as a predicate noun: 'you are a gem and wisdom by skill.'
segments.s172-g70: روانت = روان + -ت 'your soul/spirit' (possessive suffix). همی is a poetic marker meaning 'always/ever' here. از تو indicates source ('from/of you'), رامش = 'tranquility/solace', and برد is used in the sense 'gains/receives' (past-form shape but functioning idiomatically here as 'takes/gains'). Together: 'your spirit ever gains solace from you.'
segments.s172-g71: روا باشد is a fixed idiom meaning 'it is fitting/appropriate'. ار is the poetic conditional 'if'. پند من is 'my advice' (پند 'advice' with the possessor من). بشنوی is the present-subjunctive/imperative form 'you listen/heed' (ب‑ prefix + شنوی), so the clause means 'it will be fitting if you listen to my advice.'
segments.s172-g72: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/that'. آموزگار = 'teacher' and بزرگان = 'the great ones' (elders, nobles). توی is a locative/relational postposition 'in/among' (here 'among your great ones' or 'of your great ones'), so the clause says the speaker is the teacher of the addressee's great ones.
segments.s172-g57: که here is a connective 'for/that', introducing an explanatory clause. گیتی is the subject 'the world'. سپنجست is an older poetic form where سپنج (uncertain/fleeting) combines with the enclitic -ست (= 'is') to mean 'is uncertain/fleeting'. The coordinate clause و جاوید نیست places جاوید ('eternal') before the negative copula نیست ('is not'), i.e. 'and is not eternal'.
segments.s172-g58: This is a nominal predicate followed by the negative copula نیست: 'There is no ...'. فری stands as the noun being negated (here meaning the notion of farr/glory, though the provided lemma senses don't include that sense). برتر از is the comparative construction 'higher/superior than'. فر جمشید is the object of comparison 'the farr (royal glory) of Jamshid'. The clause literally: 'A farr superior to the farr of Jamshid does not exist.'
segments.s172-g59: سپهر is the noun 'sky/heavens'. بلندش is the adjective بلند 'high/lofty' with the pronominal suffix -ش 'his' attached (adjective+possessive). به پا آوردید is the verbal phrase 'to raise/erect' (به پا آوردن); آورید here is an older past-form 3rd-person plural ('they raised/brought about'). Word order places the noun phrase before the verb, as common in Persian narrative.
segments.s172-g60: جهان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker ('the world'). جزو here means 'a part/member', so جزو کدخدا آورید means 'they made (lit. brought) [the world] into the part/status of a kâdkhodâ' (i.e. reduced the world to the rank of a mere village head). آورید repeats the past 3rd-plural 'they made/brought'.
segments.s172-g61: یکی is the numeral 'one' enumerating the gift. تاج is the head noun 'crown', followed by the compound adjective پرگوهر 'full of gems/jewelled' and then شاهوار 'regal/lofty' — classical Persian often stacks adjectives after the noun. The phrase is a compact noun phrase: 'one jewelled, regal crown.'
segments.s172-g62: دو تا is a colloquial counting expression 'two (of)', here introducing the next gifts. یاره in context means 'attendants/followers' (not 'beloved'); و links the items. طوق با گوشوار is 'a collar/necklace with ear-ornaments' — طوق 'necklace/collar', با 'with', and گوشوار 'ear‑ornament/earring'.
segments.s172-g63: This clause omits an explicit subject (understood from context) and uses داد (past 'gave/granted') as the verb. سپیجاب is a place-name (uncertain in modern lexical lists) and is coordinated with سغدش: سغد + -ش (his/of him) marks Sogd as possessed/associated with the subject. به گودرز is the indirect object 'to Goudarz'. The verb داد closes the clause: 'he granted Spīnjāb and his Sogd to Goudarz.'
segments.s172-g64: بسی here is an intensifier 'much/many' in classical usage (not the modern 'بس' entry); it qualifies the following nouns. پند و منشور is a bipartite object 'counsel(s) and a charter/inscribed decree'. آن مرز is the indirect object 'to that border/frontier' (آن 'that' + مرز 'border'). داد is again the past verb 'gave' — structure: object(s) + indirect object + verb 'gave'.
segments.s172-g209: همه بوم = 'the whole land' (همه as 'whole/entire' modifying بوم). زیر و زبر is an idiomatic paired expression meaning 'upside down' or 'topsy-turvy' (زیر = 'under', زبر = 'over/above'); کرده is a past participle meaning 'made' or 'turned', and دید is the past tense verb 'he saw'. Word order is topical: the direct object phrase (همه بوم) and its adjectival/result phrase (زیر و زبر کرده) come before the verb دید.
segments.s172-g210: This is a paratactic clause with two coordinated object phrases seen by the subject: مهان کشته = 'guests killed' where کشته is a past participle/adjunct modifying مهان; کهتران برده = 'the lesser/lowly ones taken (away)' where برده is a past participle 'taken'. The trailing دید repeats the witnessing verb, echoing the previous clause.
segments.s172-g211: A classical repeated negative coordination: نه ... و نه ... lists absent items (نه اسپ و نه گنج و نه تاج و نه تخت = 'neither horse nor treasure nor crown nor throne'). The nominal items are listed without an explicit copula or verb (omission is common in poetic Persian).
segments.s172-g212: نه شاداب negates existence of freshness (شاداب = 'fresh/vigorous') applied to برگ درخت ('leaf(s) of the tree(s)'). در باغ is a locative phrase 'in the garden'. The verb 'was' is omitted; the whole line means 'not a fresh leaf remained on the garden's trees.'
segments.s172-g213: جهانی here denotes 'the whole world' (used as a noun phrase). به آتش برافروخته = 'kindled/inflamed with fire', where برافروخته is a past participle meaning 'set alight' and به marks the manner/means ('in/with fire'). The clause is a verbless or stative description common in poetry.
segments.s172-g214: همه کاخها = 'all the palaces' followed by two past participles کنده و سوخته ('torn up/removed' and 'burned'), describing their ruined state. As before, no finite verb is needed in the poetic register—the participles perform the predicative role.
segments.s172-g215: ز دیده ببارید خونابه is a poetic word order: ز دیده ('from the eye') marks the source, ببارید ('fell/poured') is the past tense verb used with liquid drops, and خونابه ('blood/gore') is the falling substance. شاه at the end is the subject ('the king'), so the whole clause reads 'blood poured from the king's eyes.'
segments.s172-g216: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (an idiomatic formula). با + NP (با مهتران سپاه) indicates 'to/with the chiefs of the army'—here marking the addressees. Note: some lemmas in the supplied list do not match the poetic forms (see senses below).
segments.s172-g1: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause here ("when"). خورشید is the clause subject; برزد is an archaic past third-person singular verb meaning "rose/appeared" or "lifted". سر از کوهسار is the noun phrase governed by the verb ('its head from the mountain-range') with از marking source; the verb precedes the small noun phrase in poetic word order.
segments.s172-g2: بگسترد is a past (archaic/poetic) verb meaning "spread/extended"; the obvious subject (the sun) is omitted and inherited from the previous clause. یاقوت functions as the material/colour spread ("ruby/ruby-red"), and بر جویبار is a prepositional phrase "upon/over the stream"; overall: "(it) spread ruby over the stream."
segments.s172-g3: تهمتن is the subject (the epithet/name of the hero). همه خواسته functions as a compact noun phrase meaning "all the requested/those who had asked" (خواسته here adjectival/nominal). گرد کرد is a verbal unit where گرد implies collective/around and کرد is the main past tense verb "made/did/assembled," so the sense is "he gathered/summoned them."
segments.s172-g4: ببخشید is a past third-person form here meaning "gave/bestowed" (not "forgave"). یکسر is an adverb "entirely/at once" modifying the verb. به introduces the indirect object; مردانِ مرد is a genitival/epithetic sequence (literally "men — man") meaning "the brave/heroic men" (i.e. "valiant men"), so the line says he distributed everything wholly to those heroes.
segments.s172-g5: خروش آمد is a light nominal clause where the noun "خروش" (a roar/uproar) takes the verb آمد "came/rose" — so: "a roar arose." و links clauses. ناله کرنای is a genitive phrase "the wailing/cry of the trumpet" (ناله = wail, کرنای = trumpet/blast).
segments.s172-g6: تهمتن is again the subject. برانگیخت is past tense (3rd sg) meaning "roused/raised" and takes لشکر as its direct object. ز جای is a prepositional phrase "from (their) place(s)" (z = from, جای = place/position), so the clause means "Tahmatan roused the army from their positions."
segments.s172-g7: نهادند is a past plural verb here ('they placed/put/turned'), formed from the root نهاد plus plural ending -ند. سر is the direct object "head(s)," سوی is the preposition "toward(s)," and افراسیاب is the object of that preposition. Together: "they turned their heads toward Afrasiab."
segments.s172-g8: همه رخ = "all faces/every face" is the subject; ز کین سیاوش is a causal/genitive phrase "from/with hatred for Siavash" (ز = from, کین = hatred/vengeance, سیاوش = the name). پر آب is a predicate-adjective sequence meaning "full of water" (a poetic way to say "filled with tears"). Word order is compressed and poetic: subject — cause — descriptive predicate.
segments.s172-g225: The verb برآراست is a past, poetic form meaning 'he arranged/arrayed (himself)'; it stands before its directional phrase, a common poetic inversion. بر here governs the noun phrase هر سوی تاختن (an infinitive clause with تاختن 'to charge'), so the whole segment means 'he made ready to charge toward every side.'
segments.s172-g226: ندید is simple past 'he did not see'; ایچ is the archaic negative/emphatic 'not at all' (equivalent to modern هیچ), and هنگام 'moment/time' takes the infinitive پرداختن as its complement. Together this clause means 'he did not see any moment for/at which to attend/turn (i.e. to stop or withdraw).' Note the infinitive here expresses the action for which no time was seen.
segments.s172-g227: همی is a frequent poetic particle indicating continuing action; سوخت is past 'burned.' آباد بوم و درخت is a stacked nominal phrase ('prosperous/inhabited land and trees'), with the verb preceding the compound subject — a normal poetic word order.
segments.s172-g228: This clause uses the light-verb construction بر شد (بر + شد) meaning 'befell' or 'wasfalling upon.' به ایرانیان marks the experiencer/recipient ('upon the Iranians'), آن کار سخت is the thing that befell them ('that hard task'). شد here functions as 'happened/befell.'
segments.s172-g229: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/for (lack of)'; باران 'rain' and هوا 'weather/air' form the topical phrase. خشک شد is past 'became dry/dried up.' The temporal span هفت سال follows to indicate duration ('for seven years').
segments.s172-g230: دگرگونه شد is 'became different/changed'; بخت 'fortune' is the subject that changes. و links to برگشت حال, where برگشت is 'return'/'reversal' and حال 'state/condition' names what was reversed — so the combined clause says that fortune altered and the situation turned about.
segments.s172-g231: شد at the start is the past 'became'; از رنج و سختی gives the cause ('from/out of suffering and hardship'), جهان is the topic ('the world' or 'mankind') and پر نیاز is a predicative phrase 'full of need.' Note the adjective پر takes the noun (نیاز) to mean 'full of X.'
segments.s172-g232: برآمد is 'arose/issued forth.' The following بر + ین = برین ('upon this') is a fused prepositional phrase meaning 'on/after this,' with روزگار دراز 'a long time/period' as the thing that arose. The word order places the verb first for poetic emphasis.
segments.s172-g217: The initial که introduces a subordinate/relative clause: 'that/whoever'. هر کس که = 'whoever' (literally 'every person who'). این را is the definite object ('this' + object marker را). فرامش کند uses فرامش (forgetfulness/forget) with کند (present/optative/formal present) to mean 'forgets' or 'should forget' in a general/habitual sense.
segments.s172-g218: همی is an archaic/adverbial marker here giving continuous/emphatic sense ('will/indeed'); جان بیدار is a noun + adjective 'the awakened soul/life'; خامش is an archaic form meaning 'silent' (parallel to modern خاموش). کند is the verb 'makes/causes' (used here as resultative: 'will cause ... to be silent'). Word order places the adverb first and the verb final, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s172-g219: همه 'all/everyone' is the addressed subject. یک به یک is an adverbial phrase 'one by one' (به used here in the adverbial sense 'by'). دل پر از کین is a noun phrase 'heart full of vengeance' where پر از = 'full of'. کنید is the 2nd-person plural imperative/formal present of کردن ('make'/'do'), giving the exhortation 'make your heart full of vengeance.'
segments.s172-g220: This is a parallel imperative clause. The nouns are juxtaposed with the omitted copula: 'سپر بستر' = 'shield (as) bed' and 'تیغ بالین' = 'sword (as) pillow/at your side'; و links the two halves. کنید (imperative) again governs the whole: 'make shield your bed and sword your pillow.' بالین here means 'pillow' or 'beside' depending on context; here 'pillow' fits the image.
segments.s172-g221: به ایران is the destination 'to Iran'. سپه رزم و کین is a coordinated noun phrase 'an army/host of battle and vengeance' (سپه = army; رزم = battle; کین = vengeance). آوریم is 1st-person plural present/subjunctive/hortative 'let us bring' or 'we will bring', giving a hortatory exhortation 'let us bring...'.
segments.s172-g222: به نیزه خور is a compound-like phrase: نیزه (spear) + خور (present stem 'eat') producing the image 'to meet/eat the spear' (i.e., to be struck by spears). اندر زمین = 'on/in the ground' (on the field). آوریم repeats the 1st-person plural verb. Overall the clause says 'let us bring them to the ground to meet the spear' (i.e., fight/defeat them on the field with spears).
segments.s172-g223: به یک رزم = 'in one battle' (a temporal/locative phrase). اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. باد here is the optative particle 'may/if it should' (classical usage) and creates a potential/contingent sense. ایشان is 'they', and بجست (literally 'leapt'/'sprang') here means 'sally forth' or 'make a sortie' — so the clause means 'if, in one battle, they happen to sally forth.'
segments.s172-g224: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one must not/it must not be that'. چنین کردن uses چنین ('such') + کردن (verbal noun/infinitive 'doing') to mean 'to do such a thing' or 'to think/act in such a way'. اندیشه پست is 'base/despicable thought/intention' (اندیشه = thought/intention; پست = base/despicable). Together: 'one should not have such base thoughts.'
segments.s172-g73: This is a locative span built with A تا B = 'from A to B' (تا here = 'as far as / up to'). The two place-nouns are given in sequence (سپیجاب ... آب گلزریون) with آب 'water' specifying the second place (the waters of Gulzarion). The proper-name گلزریون follows آب as a genitive-like name of the water.
segments.s172-g74: ز فرمان تو is an ablative/agent-like phrase 'from/under your command' (ز = poetic 'from'). کس نیاید برون is a negative jussive/optative construction: کس (anyone) before the verb, نیاید as a negative present/subjunctive meaning 'shall not/let not come', and برون 'out/forth' is the directional adverb after the verb.
segments.s172-g75: فریبرز is the grammatical subject. کاووس را uses را as a definite/topic marker attached to the recipient (a poetic dative/topic usage), and تاج زر is the noun phrase 'a crown of gold' where زر modifies تاج (golden crown). Word order places subject — recipient/topic — object phrase before the verb, which is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s172-g76: فرستاد is the main past-tense verb 'sent' (3rd sg.). The coordinated list following و — دینار و تخت و کمر — are additional direct objects enumerated after the verb; the conjunction و simply links items in the list. کمر here means 'girdle/belt.'
segments.s172-g77: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him / unto him' placed before the verb. گفت is the past verb 'said' (here the dictionary senses provided don't include the verbal sense, so sense index below is -1). سالار و مهتر توی is a coordinated subject 'the commander and the elder' followed by توی 'among you / in you' specifying their constituency; note verb precedes the nominative nouns (common in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s172-g78: This is an elliptic reported clause: سیاووش (proper name) + رد را (رد marked by را as the definite object: here 'the rejection/outcast-status of Siyavash' or 'Siyavash, the rejected one') followed by برادر توی 'your brother (among you)'. The phrase reads as an identification: 'Siyavash, the rejected one, is your brother.' Word order places the marked object phrase before the identifying phrase.
segments.s172-g79: میان را is the direct object 'the middle/gap' (را marks it). به کین برادر is a prepositional phrase 'with/by (the) brother's vengeance' where به introduces the instrument/cause. ببند is an imperative 'bind/close' addressing the addressee. Overall this is an imperative command: 'Bind/close the gap with the brother's vengeance.'
segments.s172-g80: ز فتراک is an ablative phrase 'from/off the saddle-cloth' (ز = 'from'). مگشای is a negative imperative 'do not open / do not loosen' (مـ- prefix + گشای). بند کمند is a genitive-like compound 'the band/fastening of the snare' (بند = bond/fastening, کمند = snare/lasso). The clause commands: 'Do not loosen the snare's band from the saddle-cloth.'
segments.s172-g185: 'تهمتن' is the proper name (Tahmatan) placed first for emphasis. 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'; it introduces the subordinate clause. 'بشنید' is the past third‑person perfect 'he heard'. 'شرم آمدش' is an idiomatic Persian expression literally 'shame came to him' (شرم = the feeling, آمد + ـش enclitic = 'came to him'), i.e. 'shame/embarrassment came over him.' Note the enclitic ـش on آمد marks the indirect object 'to him'.
segments.s172-g186: 'برفتن' is an infinitive meaning 'to depart/leave' here; Persian often uses an infinitive as a noun (a plan/action). 'یکی' is the indefinite 'one/a certain' qualifying 'رای' (a plan/counsel). 'رای گرم' = 'a warm/fervent counsel' where 'گرم' is an attributive adjective meaning zealously inclined. 'آمدش' again = 'came to him' (آمد + enclitic ـش). The whole phrase means 'a fervent plan to depart came to him.' Word order places the infinitive and its modifier together before the verb 'آمدش', typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s172-g187: 'نگه' is a verbal noun 'gaze/look' used with 'کرد' to form the verb 'looked' ('نگه کرد' = he looked). 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') means 'from' and 'اسپان' (horses) is the source/place ('he looked from the horses' = from the backs of his horses). 'به هر سو' = 'toward every side/every direction' (به + هر + سو). 'گله' (herd/flock) appears after the directional phrase as the direct object or focus of his look; poetic word order allows the object to follow these adverbials.
segments.s172-g188: 'که' introduces a relative clause modifying 'گله' — 'which/that'. 'بودند' is past plural 'they were'. 'بر دشت ترکان' = 'on the plain of the Turks' (بر = on, followed by noun phrase 'دشت ترکان'). 'یله' is a predicative adjective meaning 'loose/unrestrained' and follows the noun phrase it describes (typical Persian predicate position). The clause as a whole: 'which were loose on the Turks' plain.'
segments.s172-g189: This noun group lists the items: 'غلام' (slave) and 'پرستندگان' (worshippers/devotees). The numeral phrase 'ده هزار' that follows in the line (not listed as separate words here) quantifies these nouns — classical/poetic Persian can place numerals after the nouns they count. The conjunction 'و' simply links the two noun terms.
segments.s172-g190: 'بیاورد' is the past/epic perfect third person 'he brought' (classical narrative tense). 'شایسته شهریار' is a compact adjectival phrase: 'شایسته' = 'worthy/fit' and 'شهریار' = 'of/for a king' — in poetry the ezafe is often omitted but the relation is 'worthy of a king.' The verb governs the preceding noun group (he brought [those things] that are fit for a king).
segments.s172-g191: 'همان' is the demonstrative 'that/same' pointing back to previously mentioned items. 'نافه مشک' is a poetic compound: 'نافه' (here a classic poetic term, often rendered as navel or a small pendent/sachet near the navel) qualified by 'مشک' (musk) — i.e. a musk‑sachet/ornament. 'موی سمور' = 'hair/lock of sable (fur)' where the material/colour 'سمور' follows the noun 'موی' as a modifier. This is a tightly packed descriptive noun phrase typical of epic diction.
segments.s172-g192: Two prepositional/attributive fragments joined by 'و' (and). Each begins with the poetic 'ز' = 'from/of'. 'ز در سپید' = 'from the white (garment/robe/cloth)' — here 'در' functions as 'garment/robe' or part of a material phrase in poetic usage, and 'سپید' = 'white'. The next 'ز کیمال بور' likewise: 'کیمال' (perfection/complete excellence) qualified by 'بور' (fair/pale) — together a compressed description 'of flawless fair (cloth/finish)'. Poetic style often omits an explicit ezafe or noun that would be supplied by sense; these phrases describe the material/appearance of the items named earlier.
segments.s172-g169: کجا سالیان اندر آمد به شش is a compact classical clause: سالیان (the plural noun “years”) is the subject; اندر آمد (past tense) literally “came into / came to be” and takes the measure phrase به شش (“to six”) as its complement. کجا at the front is an exclamatory/interrogative adverb used here to mark extent (‘how / to what point’ or roughly ‘so many’ in poetry). Word order (adverb — subject — verb — complement) is typical of older Persian.
segments.s172-g170: که introduces the subordinate/result clause: نگذشت is the past tense with negative prefix نـ meaning “did not pass / did not occur.” بر ما is a dative-like prepositional phrase “upon/for us.” یکی روز خوش is a noun phrase ‘one (a single) happy day’ (یکی = numeral ‘one’ before روز) where خوش qualifies روز. The clause thus means ‘so that not one pleasant day passed for us.’
segments.s172-g171: کنون is an adverb ‘now.’ نزد + NP means ‘in the presence of / to’ (نزد here takes the following person as the place/recipient). آن پیر خسرو is the noun phrase ‘that aged sovereign’ (پیر as adjective ‘old/aged’ modifying خسرو ‘king/sovereign’). شویم is the 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive of شدن (‘become / be’) used here with نزد to mean ‘let us be before / let us go to’ — a classical idiom where شدن with نزد carries the sense of moving into someone's presence.
segments.s172-g172: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when.’ رزم اندر آید is a subordinate temporal clause ‘when battle comes in/when war arrives’ (آید is present/subjunctive/impersonal ‘comes’). همه نو شویم is the main clause ‘we all become new/are renewed,’ with همه ‘all’ and نو ‘new’ preceding the verb شویم (1st‑person plural, indicating the intended future/result). The sentence links a present proposal to its future outcome.
segments.s172-g173: چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause ‘when/if.’ دل is the noun ‘heart’ (object of the following verb). بر ... نهی: بر is the preposition ‘upon/on’; نهی here is an archaic verbal form meaning ‘you place / you set’ (classical subjunctive/imperative form of نهادن) rather than the nominal senses listed in the supplied lexicon, so no supplied sense fits exactly. سرای کهن is the phrase ‘the ancient house/abode’ (کهن = ancient). The clause literally: ‘when you set the heart upon the ancient house.’
segments.s172-g174: کند is the 3rd-person singular present/future of کردن ‘to do/make’ used here as an auxiliary meaning ‘it will make/do.’ ناز is a noun meaning ‘coquetry / coyness / affected pride’ and functions as the object of کند (‘it will put on coyness’). و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and.’ ز تو بپوشد سخن literally ‘it will hide speech from you’: ز indicates source/agent ‘from you,’ بپوشد is the 3rd-person singular subjunctive/present of پوشیدن used in the sense ‘to cover/conceal,’ and سخن means ‘speech/words.’
segments.s172-g175: تهمتن is a proper name (Tahmātan). بران گشت is an older verbal phrase: بران (‘to/unto that’) + گشت (past of گشتن ‘became/turned’) — together ‘turned to that / became such.’ همداستان is a predicate adjective ‘of one mind / like-minded.’ The segment says that Tahmatan became like-minded about that matter.
segments.s172-g176: که introduces reported speech or result: ‘and so/that…’ فرخنده modifies موبد and here is an epithet ‘auspicious/fortunate’ rather than a personal name. موبد is the Zoroastrian priest (mōbed). زد with این داستان is a classical idiom where زدن can mean ‘to recite/play/perform’ (not only ‘to strike’); thus زد این داستان = ‘he recited/told this tale.’ این داستان is the object ‘this story/tale.’
segments.s172-g193: A string of instrumental phrases introduced by به used in the sense 'with/by' (poetic/instrumental). Each item is a material or quality: رنگ 'color', بوی 'scent', دیبا 'brocade', زر 'gold'. The repeated به gives a rhythmic list of what they were adorned with.
segments.s172-g194: شد (past) + آراسته (past participle/adjectival) yields 'became adorned' — a typical compound where شد acts as a light verb. پشت پیلان نر is the noun phrase 'the back(s) of the male elephants' (پیلان = elephants, plural; نر postmodifies to mark 'male'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic rhythm but roles are clear: predicate then the affected noun phrase.
segments.s172-g195: ز is the poetic 'from'. گستردنیها (noun + plural suffix) refers to 'things to be spread' (e.g. carpets, coverings). از بیش و کم — 'of more and less' or 'greater and lesser' — is an idiom meaning 'of every degree/kind.' The phrase lists a category and then generalizes its range.
segments.s172-g196: Another coordinated list introduced by ز 'from': پوشیدنیها 'wearables/garments' (plural), then گنج 'treasure' and درم 'dirham(s)' (small coin). The single initial ز governs the whole series idiomatically.
segments.s172-g197: A repeated prepositional listing with ز 'from' before each major item for emphasis. گنج سلیح is a genitive construction 'treasure of arms' (an armory/weapon-treasure); then تاج 'crown' and تخت 'throne'. The repetition of ز highlights each source of booty.
segments.s172-g198: به ایران (destination) + کشیدند (3rd pl. past of کشیدن) meaning 'they drew/carried (to)' or 'they marched/took ... to Iran.' و links the next clause: بربست (past) = 'they bound/tied up' (i.e., packed) and رخت here means 'baggage/gear.' The verbs are simple past, with typical epic diction.
segments.s172-g199: ز توران 'from Turan' followed by سوی زابلستان 'toward Zabulistan' — a motion expression. کشید is the past of کشیدن used here in epic diction meaning 'moved/advanced/rolled (toward)', but none of the supplied sense options match this particular epic sense, so it is marked -1.
segments.s172-g200: به نزدیک = 'to/near' (approaching). فرخنده dستان is an epithet: فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate' modifying دستان 'hands' (here an epithet or name-like phrase 'the auspicious‑handed'); کشید again is an epic past of کشیدن 'drew near/approached' and, as above, does not match the provided sense list so is marked -1. The clause means 'he/they drew near to the Auspicious‑Handed.'
segments.s172-g33: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction ('when'/'if') introducing the clause. بدخواه is the nominative noun 'ill-wisher' (the hostile person). پیش آیدت is a verb phrase: پیش (’before/in the presence of’) + آیدت (آید + enclitic ت = 'comes to/appears to you'), so 'appears before you'. کشته is a perfect participle 'killed/slain' used predicatively (an elliptic, poetic touch); the following به is the preposition/clitic left trailing here in an archaic/elliptical construction (it links this participle to what follows or to an implied phrase).
segments.s172-g34: گر is the conditional 'if'. آواره is the noun 'wanderer/fugitive'. از پیش ('from before') is a prepositional phrase modifying برگشته (برگشته is the past participle/ perfect 'returned' or 'has returned'). The final به again appears as a trailing prepositional/clitic element in this poetic, somewhat elliptical word order, linking this protasis to what follows.
segments.s172-g35: از ایوان is a prepositional phrase 'from the iwan/porch'. همه گنج او is the direct object 'all his treasure' (همه = 'all', گنج = 'treasure', او = 'his'). بازجست is a past tense verb (3rd sg.) 'sought/searched' (classical diction; here 'searched through' or 'looked for'). Word order places the prepositional phrase up front for emphasis.
segments.s172-g36: بگفتند is a past plural verb 'they said' (or 'they spoke'). با او literally 'with him' functions idiomatically as 'to him'/'with him' (they addressed him). یکایک is an adverbial 'one by one' / 'each and every', and درست here is an adverb/adjectival sense 'rightly/straightforwardly' (i.e. 'each one spoke plainly/true'). The clause describes how the speakers addressed him, with one-by-one frankness.
segments.s172-g37: A coordinated noun phrase listing three plural items: غلامان (servants), اسپ (steeds/ horses), and پرستندگان (attendants/worshippers). The conjunction و simply joins the items; there is no verb here—the existence/state is implied by the following segment. Word order is the simple list order common in Persian poetry.
segments.s172-g38: همان is a demonstrative/determiner linking back to something previously mentioned ("that same"); مایه‌ور is an adjectival compound (مایه + -ور) meaning "rich/wealthy" and modifies the following nominal phrase. خوب رخ is a two-word descriptive phrase ('good/beautiful' + 'face/appearance') giving the attribute 'fair‑faced'; بندگان is the head noun ('servants/people') that the modifiers attach to. There is no copula; the clause asserts that the listed beings belonged to this same rich, fair‑faced class.
segments.s172-g39: در + گنج marks the locative phrase 'in the treasure'. What follows is a noun list of contents: دینار (money/coins) and پرمایه تاج ('a crown rich in substance' — پرمایه functioning as an adjective before تاج). Persian often omits a verb of existence, so the phrase implies 'were' or 'there was' in English.
segments.s172-g40: همان here is a resumptive/demonstrative ('the same') tying these items to what was just mentioned. The following coordinated nouns گوهر (gems), دیبه (fine silk/brocade or silken robe), and تخت عاج (literally 'throne [of] ivory' — تخت = throne, عاج = ivory, with the material following the noun) enumerate further treasures. Again, no copula is expressed; existence is implied.
segments.s172-g105: بدین جایگه = a prepositional/locative phrase ‘in this place’ (بدین = به + این in archaic/poetic spelling). شاد و خرم are two predicative adjectives ‘happy and joyful’ that describe the subject. بدی here is an archaic/poetic past 2nd-person form (بودی → بدی) meaning ‘you were’; word order places the locative phrase first for emphasis and the adjectives before the verb.
segments.s172-g106: جز ایدر همه جای = ‘except here, everywhere’ (جز = except; ایدر = here; همه جای = the whole place / everywhere). با غم = an ablative/with-phrase ‘with grief/sorrow’ modifying the state. بدی again is the archaic past 2nd-person copula ‘you were’ (بودی → بدی). The clause asserts contrast: only in this spot was he happy; elsewhere he was sorrowful.
segments.s172-g107: چو = ‘when’. زواره is a proper name (Zavāreh) and is the subject. بشنید is past tense ‘he heard’. ز + و here is the poetic ‘از او’ = ‘from him’ (z + [o/u] = ‘from him’). این سخن = ‘this speech’ is the object. Word order places the verb before its object because of the subordinating چو-clause and poetic inversion.
segments.s172-g108: برو is a short discourse/temporal particle ‘thereupon/then’ in classical usage (not the literal imperative ‘go’). تازه شد = past ‘became fresh/new’ (تازه predicate, شد past of شدن). روزگار کهن = ‘the old/ancient time/age’ (subject). Typical Persian SOV is flexed for poetic order: the verb phrase تازه شد precedes the subject روزگار کهن to highlight the change.
segments.s172-g109: چو = ‘when’. گفتار آن ترکش = ‘the speech/words of that Turk’; ترکش is ترک + ـش (possessive enclitic) = ‘that Turk’s’. آمد به گوش = a common idiom ‘(it) came to (his) ear’ = ‘reached his ear / he heard’. The clause is a temporal subordinate introduced by چو with a past tense verb بشنید/آمد.
segments.s172-g110: ز اسپ اندر افتاد = ‘he fell from the horse’ (ز = from, اسپ = horse, اندر often adds a sense of inward/downwards/into the ground, افتاد = fell). و = ‘and’ links the two result clauses. زو رفت هوش = poetic phrasing for ‘his awareness went from him’ (زو = ز + او = from him; رفت = went/left; هوش = awareness/consciousness) — i.e., he lost consciousness.
segments.s172-g111: یکی = an indefinite ‘one / a certain’. باز here is the noun ‘falcon/hawk’ (classical diction). بودش = بود + ـش (‘it was’/‘there was to him’—the enclitic signals possession or existence with respect to him). به چنگ = ‘in (his) grip/hand’ (چنگ = talon/handful), اندرون = ‘within / inside’ (adverbial). The whole phrase says ‘a falcon was in his grasp.’
segments.s172-g112: رها کرد = past ‘he released/let loose (it)’ (رها = release, کرد = did/made). و = ‘and’. مژگان شدش جوی خون = literally ‘his eyelashes became a stream of blood’ (مژگان = eyelashes; شدش = شد + ـش = ‘it became to him/befell him’; جوی خون = ‘a stream of blood’). The predicate is placed after the subject in a poetic inversion: the effect (جوی خون) is said to have befallen his مژگان.
segments.s172-g177: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' placed before the speaker (a common poetic word order: 'thus said X'); خرم دل is an adjectival noun phrase 'joyful-hearted' (خرم modifying دل) describing the speaker; رهنمای is the noun 'guide' (the agent of گفت).
segments.s172-g178: که introduces the reported content ('that'); خوبی گزین is an imperative clause with گزین as the jussive/imperative 'choose' and خوبی as its object 'goodness' or 'the good'; زین ... سرای is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'from this ... abode' where سپنجی is an adjective 'sponge-like / spongy' modifying سرای 'abode/house' (a poetic/archaic meaning of سرای rather than 'sing/compose').
segments.s172-g179: This is a string of coordinated imperatives: بنوش, بناز, بپوش, بخور — each verb bears the classical imperative/jussive sense (the prefixed ب- is typical in older Persian forms). The conjunction و connects the actions ('and').
segments.s172-g180: ترا here is the dative/beneficiary 'to you' (written in the data as ت + را); بهره thisست is the copular clause 'this is your share/portion' with اینست as the copula 'this is'; زین رهگذر is an adverbial/ablative phrase 'from this passerby/wayfarer' (زین = 'from this' in archaic/poetic usage, modifying رهگذر).
segments.s172-g181: سوی functions as 'toward' and the verb منگر is an archaic negative imperative 'do not look' (م- + نگَر); که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause 'for he is an enemy' (او دشمنست), giving the reason not to turn toward greed (آز).
segments.s172-g182: دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش); برده is a past participle/adjective 'taken/captured' describing دلش; جان آهرمنست is a nominal clause reading 'his life/soul is Ahriman' or more idiomatically 'his soul belongs to Ahriman' (آهرمن/اهریمن = the evil spirit).
segments.s172-g183: نگه کن is an imperative 'look!/pay attention!' composed of نگه (gaze/watch) plus the auxiliary/imperative کن 'do'; که begins the content clause 'that/who...'; در خاک indicates location 'in the earth' (i.e., among the dead or buried); جفت تو 'your mate/spouse' followed by کیست 'who is' (inverted word order typical of poetry).
segments.s172-g184: برین is a fused prepositional phrase 'بر این' = 'about/on this'; خواسته is 'wish/desire' (the thing being mourned); چند خواهی گریست is a rhetorical question 'how long will you weep?' where چند = 'how long/how much', خواهی is the 2nd-person future/auxiliary 'you will', and گریست is 'weep/lament.'
segments.s172-g153: ندانیم is the negative present/past-stem first‑person plural/poetic form meaning “we do not know” (ن‑ prefix + دانیم). The following noun phrase ماکان جفاگر is the thing not known (the cruel/tyrannical Mākān), and کجاست is an embedded interrogative copular clause (‘where is …’), with کجا ‘where’ followed by ست (is). Word order places the main verb of knowing (ندانیم) before the subordinate interrogative clause.
segments.s172-g154: This clause gives two location‑images as alternatives: به ابرست‌گر is a prepositional phrase (به + a rare/epithet אبرست‌گر, literally “to/with the cloud‑raiser” or ‘up with the clouds’), and در دم اژدهاست places someone ‘in the dragon’s breath’ (در = in, دم = breath, اژدها = dragon). The two locative phrases are juxtaposed to indicate possible whereabouts; the verb ‘is’ is carried by the embedded کجاست in the previous segment.
segments.s172-g155: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. بشنید is past third‑person singular ‘he heard’. گفتار آن انجمن is the object phrase ‘the speech/words of that assembly’ (گفتار = speech; آن modifies انجمن). In classical word order the temporal clause precedes the main result clause that follows.
segments.s172-g156: بپیچید is a past form of پیچیدن with the prefix ب‑, here meaning ‘(was) turned/twisted’ or ‘was thrown into turmoil’ (intransitive result). بینادل is an uncertain/rare reading preserved in the text (lexical meaning unclear), and پیلتن is a place‑name (Pilten); together the phrase indicates that the place (Binadel Pilten) was shaken/turned. The verb is intransitive and agrees with the scene/place as subject.
segments.s172-g157: سوی مرز قچغار is a fronted prepositional phrase ‘toward the Qachghar border’. باشی here functions as a noun/title (the bāshī, i.e. a commander/official sent) or as the person sent (the exact medieval sense is idiomatic), and براند is past third‑person singular of راندن ‘to drive/send’ (‘he sent/drove’). The verb at the end follows the typical Persian V‑final order; the phrase as a whole means he dispatched someone toward that frontier.
segments.s172-g158: سران سپه را is the definite object ‘the leaders of the army’ with را marking the direct object. سراسر is an adverb meaning ‘entirely/throughout’ (here ‘all together’), and بخواند is past third‑person singular of خواندن used in the sense ‘to call/summon’. Together: ‘he summoned all the army‑leaders’.
segments.s172-g159: شدند is the past plural of شدن ‘to become/come to be’ used here to mean ‘they gathered/assembled’. انجمن is the assembly/meeting; پیش او is the locative phrase ‘before him/in his presence’; بخردان is an appositive/adjectival plural ‘the wise ones’ (بخرد = wise). The verb precedes the noun phrase in the poetic ordering but the meaning is that an assembly gathered in his presence composed of the wise.
segments.s172-g160: This is a nominative appositive list detailing who was present: بزرگان ‘the great ones/leaders’ and (و) کارآزموده ‘the experienced’, followed by ردان (an epithet/name for a group/person). The nouns are coordinated; the phrase enumerates the eminent, experienced people who composed the assembly.
segments.s172-g25: به نزدیک افراسیاب آمدند is an adverbial + verb clause: به نزدیک (literary order) = 'near / toward' Afrasiab, followed by آمدند (3rd-person plural past) 'they came'. The prepositional phrase precedes the verb in this elevated register.
segments.s172-g26: پر از درد و تیمار و تاب آمدند is a predicative phrase describing the manner of arrival: پر از + noun = 'full of ...'. آمدند repeats the past plural verb for emphasis. The three items joined by و form a noun-list of states (pain, care/worry, and physical weakness/endurance).
segments.s172-g27: وز آن جایگه شاه توران زمین is an elevated, slightly inverted noun phrase: وز (archaic/poetic linker here meaning 'from' — not present in the provided sense list) + آن جایگه 'that place', then شاه توران زمین 'the king of Turan (land)'. شاه توران is a genitival/name phrase and زمین specifies 'land/country'.
segments.s172-g28: بیاورد لشکر به دریای چین contains the verb بیاورد (3rd-sg. perfective/literary: 'brought') with its direct object لشکر 'army' following it in this poetic word order; به + place (دریای چین) marks the destination: 'to the Sea of China'.
segments.s172-g29: تهمتن نشست از بر تخت اوی: تهمتن is the epic epithet/name (here used as subject); نشست is past 'sat/rose' (in older Persian نشست can mean 'sat' or 'took a seat/rose' depending on context); از بر تخت اوی is an archaic locative phrase meaning 'from off his throne' (از = off/from, بر = on/upon, اوی = his).
segments.s172-g30: به خاک اندر آمد سر بخت اوی is a semi-idiomatic clause: به خاک اندر آمد 'came into the dust/ground' = 'fell/prostrated' and سر بخت اوی is a genitive phrase 'the head of his fortune' (سر = head, بخت = fortune/fate, اوی = his). The verb آمد governs the whole image: his fortune's head came down to the dust.
segments.s172-g31: یکی داستانی بگفت از نخست: یکی here = 'a certain one / someone'; داستانی = 'a tale/story' (noun); بگفت is the literary past 'he told/said'; از نخست = 'from the first / from the beginning', indicating the tale begins at the outset.
segments.s172-g32: که پرمایه آنکس که دشمن نجست is a pair of embedded relative clauses: the first که introduces 'that' (linking to 'داستانی'), پرمایه آنکس = 'that substantial/rich person' (پرمایه as an epithet), then a second که introduces 'who' + دشمن نجست where نجست (root جَست) here means 'to seek / look for' so دشمن نجست = 'who did not seek (an) enemy' (i.e. had no enemy or did not go seeking enemies).
segments.s172-g201: This is a poetic word order where the predicate 'شد' (past of شدن) and the direction 'سوی پارس' come before the subjects. Here 'سوی پارس شد' functions as 'turned/moved toward Pars' with the names طوس، گودرز و گیو as the grammatical subjects that follow; 'شد' is the past tense verb applied to those names.
segments.s172-g202: سپاهی is a noun 'army/host' modified by the adjectival phrase 'چنان نامبردار و نیو' — 'چنان' = 'such' qualifies the following adjectives. 'نامبردار' = 'renowned' and is joined by 'و' to 'نیو', here used as an adjective 'new/recent' (regional/poetic). The whole phrase describes the army that the named heroes bring.
segments.s172-g203: نهادند is a third-person plural past tense of نهادن 'to place/put' used here idiomatically as 'they bent/turned' (their heads). The direct object 'سر' ('head') follows the verb, and the prepositional direction 'سوی شاه جهان' ('toward the King of the World') indicates the target of the action.
segments.s172-g204: همه is a universal quantifier 'all' modifying نامداران ('renowned/illustrious ones'). The two following words فرخ and نهان are adjectival modifiers attached to نامداران (Persian often places adjectives after the noun in poetry): فرخ = 'fortunate' and نهان = 'hidden/secret', so the noun phrase reads 'all the illustrious, fortunate, and concealed.'
segments.s172-g205: This temporal clause opens with وزان پس چو — a sequence marker ('and from that/after that, when') leading into the main verb. بشنید is past tense 'he heard' (3rd person), and افراسیاب is the subject performing the hearing. The cluster sets up a 'when Afrasiab heard' subordinate clause.
segments.s172-g206: The particle که introduces reported content ('that/which'): بگذشت is a past intransitive verb 'passed/crossed' referring to رستم as subject. بران is a deictic compound (بر + آن) meaning 'on that/thereon', and روی آب literally 'the surface of the water' — together ‘had crossed that stretch/on that surface of the water.’
segments.s172-g207: Here شد functions as a simple past verbal predicate 'he set out / went' (poetic usage). The phrase از باختر سوی دریای گنگ is a directional adverbial: از + باختر = 'from the west', سوی = 'toward', followed by the object 'دریای گنگ' ('the sea of Gang/Ganges' in the poem). Word order places the verb before the adverbial for poetic effect.
segments.s172-g208: This is a paired nominal description of inner state: دلی پر ز کینه = 'a heart full of hatred' and سری پر ز جنگ = 'a head full of war.' The construction 'پر ز' means 'full of/of' (پر + the preposition ز 'of'), so two parallel noun phrases describe his feelings and intentions.
segments.s172-g17: هم آنگه is a short temporal/transition phrase (هم = 'also/then', آنگه = 'then'), placing what follows immediately after the previous action. برندش is a poetic/older-form verbal clause meaning 'they took/carried him' (third-person plural action) and is followed by the prepositional phrase به ایران سپاه 'to Iran, the army' (به + place + noun). Word order is subject/agent omitted (understood from context), verb then destination.
segments.s172-g18: یکی ناسزا برنهندش کلاه is an inverted poetic order: یکی ('a certain' / 'a') modifies ناسزا ('an insult'), برنهندش is a causative/old-form verb 'they put upon/placed on him', and کلاه ('cap') is the thing placed. Literally 'they placed upon him a certain insulting cap' — i.e. a humiliating mark. The object (کلاه) appears at the end as often in Persian.
segments.s172-g19: نوندی is a proper name here (a person who acts), followed by the verbal root برافگن, an archaic/poetic form meaning 'to throw/uproot/utter' (used here in the sense 'to hurl' or 'to fling' — i.e., insults or cries). هم اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'also/at that time,' marking simultaneity or immediacy; the verb phrase governs what will follow.
segments.s172-g20: بر شوم پی‌زاده بدگمان is a nominal/qualifying phrase that the verb affects: بر 'against/on' + شوم 'ill‑omened' (an adjective) + پی‌زاده 'offspring/son' + بدگمان 'mistrusted/suspicious'. Together it yields 'against the ill‑omened, mistrusted offspring.' The adjectives precede the noun, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s172-g21: که introduces a purpose/subordinate clause here ('so that' / 'in order that'). با مادر آن هر دو تن را به هم is a prepositional cluster: با مادر آن 'with their mother' (آن = 'that/the'), هر دو تن را 'both persons' with the object marker را, and به هم 'together' (lit. 'to each other'). The clause sets up the intended action: bringing the two people together with their mother.
segments.s172-g22: بیارد بگوید سخن بیش و کم contains two verbs in sequence in poetic/older forms: بیارد (here a poetic form meaning 'bring' / 'cause to come together') followed by بگوید ('to say' / 'he may say'), then the direct object سخن 'words' and the adverbial phrase بیش و کم 'more or less' (i.e. some words, of varying amount). Note: the provided lemma/sense lists for بیارد and بگوید mismatch the expected verbal senses, so they are treated as verbal here.
segments.s172-g23: نوندی بیامد ببردندشان is a coordination of actions: نوندی (proper name) + بیامد 'came' (simple past) followed by ببردندشان 'they took them away / carried them off' (a past/perfective plural verb). The sequence describes arrival then removal — typical narrative past ordering.
segments.s172-g24: شدند آن دو بیچاره چون بیهشان: شدند is the past plural of 'become/happen' ('they became' / 'they were'), آن دو بیچاره 'the two wretches' is the subject, and چون بیهشان is a simile/complaint phrase ('as if without them' / 'left without (support)') in archaic phrasing. Overall it states the two were left miserable/helpless after the action.
segments.s172-g81: به introduces a direction/destination ('into/to') followed by the place names چین و ختن; اندرآور is an imperative/command 'bring in' (classical literary form) and سپاه is its direct object that follows the verb here. Word order is Destination — Verb — Object, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s172-g82: به هر جای is a prepositional phrase 'to every place' (به + quantifier هر + جای). از links that place to دشمنان (literally 'place of the enemies'), and کینه‌خواه is a postpositive descriptor 'vengeful' modifying دشمنان. Overall it marks the targets of the previous command.
segments.s172-g83: میاسای is an exhortative/optative form meaning 'be at rest/cease' (classical diction). از کین is the prepositional phrase 'from hatred', and افراسیاب is the proper name (here vocative/identifier) associated with the exhortation.
segments.s172-g84: ز تن دور کن is an imperative clause: ز (poetic 'from') + تن 'the body' + دور کن 'make distant/keep away' (کن = imperative 'do'). خورد is used here in the sense of 'that which befalls' or 'harm that may befall' (a nominalized/becoming form), followed by a list آرام و خواب ('calm and sleep') — a sequence of desired states. Grammatically the imperative governs the following nouns as what should be kept away or granted.
segments.s172-g85: به ماچین و چین is the prepositional phrase 'to Machin and Chin' marking the recipients of the report; آمد is the past-tense verb 'came' placed before the subject (a poetic inversion), and این آگهی is the logical subject 'this news/report'.
segments.s172-g86: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'. بنشست is past tense 'sat (down / took a seat)' with رستم as the subject; به شاهنشهی is a prepositional phrase indicating position or office — 'on/into the kingship/imperial throne' (شاهنشهی is a literary noun 'kingship/imperial rule').
segments.s172-g87: همه is the universal subject 'all/everyone'. هدیه‌ها is the plural 'gifts/offerings'. ساختند is the past-tense plural verb 'they made/produced', and نثار ('offering') follows as a coordinating noun/verb meaning 'and offered' — a compact poetic coordination: they made and offered gifts.
segments.s172-g88: ز before materials here means 'in/from' (a poetic partitive) — 'of dinars and of gems'. گوهر 'gem' is qualified by the postpositive adjective شاهوار 'regal/lofty' (an elevated adjective used in courtly diction). The phrase specifies the material/quality of the gifts.
segments.s172-g161: که is a connective/relative ('that/who') linking this clause to the previous thought. کاووس is the subject (the name Kavus). The sequence بی‌دست و بی فر و پای is a coordinated descriptive phrase: بی- is the privative prefix 'without', so بی‌دست 'without hand' here = 'powerless', بی فر 'without farr/royal glory' (فر is the archaic notion of royal radiance), and پای 'foot/base/support' (here meaning 'support' or 'standing'). The adjectives follow the noun in a piled, poetic fashion common in classical Persian.
segments.s172-g162: نشستست is an archaic verbal form meaning 'is seated / sat' (third person); the -ست ending is classical. بر تخت is the prepositional phrase 'on the throne' (بر = on; تخت = throne). بی‌رهنمای is an adjectival/phrase 'without a guide' literally 'unguided' and describes the circumstance of his sitting (he sits on the throne unguided). Word order (verb then prepositional phrase then modifying adjective) reflects classical/persian poetic syntax.
segments.s172-g163: گر is the conditional 'if'. افراسیاب is the subject name. از + رهی functions adverbially as 'by/from a route' (رهی here = 'on the road / by a way' in a somewhat elliptical classical form). بی‌درنگ is an adverb ('without delay / immediately') that will modify the following verb.
segments.s172-g164: یکی لشکر آرد is a verbal phrase meaning 'bring(s) one/ an army' — یکی here functions as 'one / a single' (i.e. 'an army'), لشکر is 'army', and آرد (classical present/subjunctive form of آوردن) 'brings' or 'should bring' in the conditional context. به ایران is the goal 'into Iran' (به + place = into/toward), and به جنگ is the purpose 'to (make) war' (a standard collocation meaning 'to go to battle').
segments.s172-g165: بیابد is a classical form of یافتن meaning 'may obtain / will get' (subjunctive/optative sense here because of the conditional context). The following nominal phrase is its object: بران ('upon that' / 'thereon') + پیر ('old man') and then کاووس دست = 'Kavus's hand/power' (دست here means 'power/control' rather than literally 'hand'). The whole chunk means 'that old man will obtain Kavus's power/control.'
segments.s172-g166: شود is the verbal auxiliary 'becomes / will be' used to introduce the result clause. کام و آرام ما is the affected noun phrase 'our desire and peace/repose' (کام = wish/desire; آرام = calm/rest). جمله functions as an adverbial 'all / altogether' modifying ما پست: پست ('low / abased') predicated of 'ما' — the line states that our wishes and rest will come about and that we shall all be humbled. Word order places the verb before the affected nouns in a compact poetic structure.
segments.s172-g167: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn'. همه modifies the following noun as 'every / all'. فام here (classical usage) refers to 'lineage / trace' (given senses offered as 'hue' or 'trace', the sense 'trace/lineage' fits the context), and کین 'vengeance' is the object/manner. توختیم is a classical past-tense first-person-plural form — an archaic verb meaning we struck/dealt with (here 'wreaked' or 'dealt vengeance'). The clause thus says 'one by one we exacted vengeance on every line/trace.'
segments.s172-g168: همه شهر آباد او is a noun phrase 'all the (prosperous/inhabited) towns/cities of his' — note the possessive pronoun او follows the noun in classical style. سوختیم is the past first-person-plural of سوزدن 'to burn / to be burned' and here means 'we burned' (active past). The clause states that they burned all his flourishing cities.
segments.s172-g89: تهمتن is the proper name subject. به جان داد is an idiomatic cluster: جان (life/soul) with به marks the notion of 'giving up one's life' and داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' (here 'sacrificed/gave up'); زنهارشان (زنهار + the pronominal suffix -شان) is the object 'their warning/admonition'. Word order is subject — verb phrase — object, typical of classical Persian where the object can follow the verb and carry the possessive suffix.
segments.s172-g90: بدید is a past-tense verb 'saw' (the b- prefix appears in older/poetic past forms). آن is a demonstrative 'that/those' (here 'those'). روانهای is the plural form 'spirits/souls' and بیدارشان is the adjective 'awake' with the pronominal suffix -شان 'their', so the phrase means 'he saw those (their) awake spirits.' The adjective follows the noun and the possessive suffix attaches to the adjective here.
segments.s172-g91: وزان پس is a temporal connector 'and/from that (time) then/after that'. بنخچیر is an object meaning 'prey, spoils' (the result of hunting). با introduces the instruments or companions 'with', followed by یوز (a hunting animal, here 'cheetah' or similar coursing hound) and باز ('falcon' / hunting hawk). The list shows the means by which they obtained the prey.
segments.s172-g92: برآمد is an intransitive past 'arose' / 'came into being'. برین is the fusion of بر + این ('upon/after this' = 'following that'); روزگاری دراز is a noun phrase 'a long period' (روزگاری = 'a time/period', دراز = 'long') where adjective follows noun. Together: 'a long period arose/followed.'
segments.s172-g93: چنان ... که is a formula meaning 'so (it was) that ...' — چنان = 'so/thus'. The following words give the event: روزی normally means 'one day' (a temporal adverb), زواره is a place or proper name (here the person/place that 'set out'), and برفت is a past verb 'went away / set out' (the b- prefixed past common in epic style). The clause means 'so it happened that one day Zowāreh went off.'
segments.s172-g94: به نخچیر is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the hunt/prey' (به + نخچیر). گوران here names a group (the Gūrān) or may be a genitive/agent in context; it is the noun that accompanies the hunting sense. خرامید is the past verb 'strode/pranced/advanced proudly' (intransitive). تفت is unclear in the supplied sense list (could be a place-name or adverbial); in this line it functions adverbially or as a place toward which the subject strode. Overall: 'he went to the hunt; the Guran strode proudly (toward T[a]ft/there).'
segments.s172-g95: یکی ترک = 'a certain Turk' (یکی = 'a certain/someone', ترک = 'Turk' here a person). تا introduces a purpose or result clause 'so that / in order that'. باشدش is باشد (present/subjunctive 'may be / be') with the enclitic -ش 'for him' (i.e., 'may be for him' / 'be his'), and رهنمای is the predicate noun 'guide'. The clause expresses purpose: 'a certain Turk so that he might be his guide.'
segments.s172-g96: به پیش اندر افگند is built from the prepositional phrase به پیش 'into the presence / before (someone)' + اندر (in/into) + افگند (past of افکندن 'to throw/cast'). Literally 'cast (himself/him) into the presence'; the object can be implicit. و آمد بجای is a coordinating clause 'and came in place' — آمد 'came' and بجای 'in the place / in his stead' (here 'took his place'). The two clauses together mean he threw himself forward into the presence and took the place (i.e., interposed himself as guide).
segments.s172-g129: چرا ('why') begins the rhetorical question; باید is an epistemic/obligative modal 'must/ought' which here governs the verb that follows; این کشور آباد ماند is an older/poetic ordering (instead of modern آباد بماند) where ماند (present/simple stem of ماندن) expresses continuing existence ('remain/continue to be'), so the clause literally asks why this country should continue to be prosperous.
segments.s172-g130: یکی را is 'one person' with را used as a topical/emphatic marker; برین is the poetic contraction of بر این ('on this') where ین is the orthographic/poetic form of این, so برین بوم = 'on this soil'; و links the two halves and بر شاد ماند uses the same verbal form ماند ('remain') to mean 'remain/continue (to be) happy.'
segments.s172-g131: فرامش مکن is the negative imperative 'do not forget' (literally 'do not make forgetfulness'); آن شهریار = 'that king.' The intervening کین here is an archaic/poetic particle (see note below) introducing the following clause—treat it as a fixed classical usage rather than the modern noun 'vengeance.'
segments.s172-g132: که introduces the subordinate (relative/explanatory) clause 'for/since'; چون = 'like/as'; او = 'he'; نبیند is the negative present form of دیدن ('does not/will not see' or 'will see not') used here in a classical sense to mean the world will not see another like him; دگر روزگار = 'another time/age' (روزگار = 'times/age'), so the clause explains why one should not forget the king.
segments.s172-g133: برانگیخت is the simple past 'roused' or 'stirred up' and appears before its object for poetic inversion; آن پیلتن را is the definite object ('that Piltan', with را marking the definite object); ز جای = 'from its place' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from').
segments.s172-g134: تهمتن (Tahmēntan) is the subject; هم 'also'/ 'likewise' qualifies his action; آن کرد = 'did that' (آن = 'that [deed]'; کرد = past 'did'); کاو is a clipped/archaic form corresponding to که او ('which he' / 'that he'), introducing the relative clause; دید رای = 'saw (دید) the counsel/plan (رای)' — i.e. he carried out what he judged to be the plan.
segments.s172-g135: همان 'that very' points back to what was described; غارت و کشتن = a noun phrase 'plundering and killing/slaughter'; اندر گرفت uses گرفت (past 'took/seized' or 'began') with اندر 'in' to mean 'took up/began (that) plundering and killing.'
segments.s172-g136: همه بوم و بر = 'all the land and dwellings' (بوم و بر is a stock pairing meaning realm and settlements); دست بر سر گرفت is an idiomatic sequence 'to lay hands upon the head(s)' or 'to seize/punish' where گرفت (past) completes the action — overall: he seized/attacked every place.
segments.s172-g9: پس is a sequencing adverb ('then'). آگاهی is a noun meaning 'news/information' and آمد is the past verb 'came/reached'. به introduces the recipient ('to') and پرخاشجوی is the recipient noun 'the quarrelsome/hostile one'. Word order (Adverb + Noun + Verb + prepositional recipient) reflects poetic flexibility but reads: 'then information came to the quarrelsome one'.
segments.s172-g10: که here introduces the content clause ('that ...'). رستم is the subject. به + توران expresses direction ('toward Turan'). در آورد روی is a verbal phrase: در (part of the compound) + آورد (past 'brought/turned') + روی ('face') combine idiomatically to mean 'turned his face/took his turn toward'. The verb آمد/آورد sequence and the split elements reflect older/poetic phrasing.
segments.s172-g11: به پیران is the indirect object 'to the elders'. چنین گفت is 'said thus/said so' with گفت (past 'said'); in classical/poetic Persian the subject often follows the verb, so کایرانیان (the Kayanians) is placed after گفت rather than before it. Note: گفت functions here as past-tense 'said' (poetic omission of -ند for plural is common).
segments.s172-g12: بدی is the definite object 'the evil/wrongdoing' and را marks it as such. ببستند is the past, 3rd-person plural ('they shut/bound/put an end to'); یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether'; میان means 'between' (locative). The clause reads literally 'they entirely put an end to the evil between (them)'.
segments.s172-g13: کنون is 'now'. بوم و بر is a paired phrase meaning 'homeland and land' (a fixed poetic collocation). جمله is an intensifier 'all/entire', placed before the adjective. ویران شود uses شود (present/subjunctive form used here as future passive) to mean 'will be ruined/laid waste'. Overall: 'Now the homeland and land will all be ruined.'
segments.s172-g14: به کام is an idiom meaning 'to the desire/advantage of'. دلیران ایران is a genitival phrase 'the brave ones of Iran' (in poetry the ezāfe can be omitted). شود repeats the copular/passive sense 'will be' and links this result to the previous clause: the ruin will be to the satisfaction/benefit of Iran's warriors.
segments.s172-g15: کسی is 'someone' (indefinite subject). نزد رستم means 'to/into the presence of Rostam' (the beneficiary/recipient). برد is the past of بردن here meaning 'brought' (poetic past form). آگهی is the object 'news/information'. Word order (subject + prepositional phrase + verb + object) is a poetic arrangement for 'someone brought news to Rostam.'
segments.s172-g16: ازین = از + این ('of/from this') where ین is the orthographic/poetic variant of این; together it means 'concerning this'. کودک is 'child'. شوم is an adjective 'ill‑omened/unlucky' and بی‌فرهی ('without learning') modifies the child as 'unlearned/ignorant'. The whole prepositional phrase modifies آگهی: 'news concerning this ill‑omened, unlearned child.'
segments.s172-g137: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the range. 'توران زمین' is a genitival or attributive sequence meaning 'the land of Turan' (noun + زمین 'land'). 'تا به' together mark the limit 'as far as / up to' and is followed by the place names 'سقلاب و روم'. Word order is typical poetic Persian: preposition + place phrase + 'تا به' + destination.
segments.s172-g138: نماندند is a past plural intransitive verb 'there were not left / remained' (negative sense implicit in context). It takes the following nominal phrase 'یک مرز آباد بوم' as what did not remain; 'یک' = 'one/any', 'مرز' = 'border/frontier', 'آباد' = 'inhabited/prosperous' (adjectival), 'بوم' = 'homeland'. The verb precedes its nominal complement, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s172-g139: همی is a narrative adverb meaning 'kept on / continuously' here, attached to the action. 'سر بریدند' is a past plural transitive verb phrase 'they cut off heads / they beheaded'. The objects 'برنا و پیر' ('the young and the old') follow the verb; Persian word order is flexible in poetry but here the verb+object expresses repeated violent action.
segments.s172-g140: This clause lists further victims: 'زن و کودک خرد' = 'women and small children' (خرد functions as an adjective 'small'). 'کردند' is the past plural of 'کردن' meaning 'they made' and is followed by the predicative noun 'اسیر' ('captives'), so the structure is Obj + verb + object-complement: they made (those) women and little children captives.
segments.s172-g141: 'برین گونه' is an archaic/poetic phrase meaning 'in this manner / thus' (بر + این -> برین + گونه 'way'). The measure phrase 'فرسنگ بیش از هزار' literally places the unit 'farsang' before the comparative 'بیش از هزار' ('more than a thousand'); this inverted order is poetic but reads as 'more than a thousand farsangs.'
segments.s172-g142: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth'. 'ز کشور' is a source prepositional phrase 'from the country/realm'. 'سراسر دمار' functions as an adverbial/nominal phrase meaning 'utter ruin throughout' or 'ruin everywhere'—together the clause says that vast destruction sprang up throughout the land.
segments.s172-g143: هرآنکس که begins a relative clause 'whoever / anyone who'. 'بد مهتری' is a compound meaning roughly 'ill (i.e., blameworthy or arrogant) preeminence' or 'badly asserted superiority'; 'با گهر' ('with jewels') is an instrumental/associative phrase modifying that prideful preeminence. The clause describes people prideful in rank and ornament.
segments.s172-g144: 'همه' is the subject 'all/everyone'. 'پیش رفتند' is the past plural 'they went forward / they advanced', here idiomatically 'they were brought low' or 'fell'; 'بر خاک سر' is a prepositional phrase literally 'upon the earth (head)'—an image meaning 'fell headlong to the ground'. Word order places the verb before the locative phrase, as common in poetry.
segments.s172-g41: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' that opens the clause. ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side' (ز + هر سو). به چنگ آمدش is a verbal phrase: به چنگ 'into the grasp' (به = towards/into; چنگ = claw/hand, here 'grasp') and آمدش is the past-form verb آمد + enclitic -ش 'to him', literally 'came to him' (so the whole phrase = 'came into his grasp').
segments.s172-g42: بسی here functions as a classical quantifier 'many' (classical usage not fully captured by the listed senses) modifying گوهر 'gems'. از گنج گنگ آمدش literally 'came to him from the (gank) treasure': از = 'from', گنج = 'treasure/treasure-house' and گنگ is an epithet (here 'gank/gloomy/hidden' or 'muffled' in the lexicon) modifying گنج; آمدش again is آمد + -ش 'came to him'.
segments.s172-g43: سپه is the subject 'the army/host'. سر به سر is a repetitive idiom here meaning 'one and all' or 'from head to toe/throughout' (the two سر's form an adverbial phrase; به is the usual preposition linking them). زان = ز + آن 'from that / because of that', and توانگر شدند is توانگر 'wealthy' + شدند 'they became' (past/pluperfect sense 'became' or 'came to be').
segments.s172-g44: A nominal list serves as the predicate of شدند: ابا یاره و تخت و افسر شدند — a series of nouns joined by و ('and') that they 'became' or 'acquired'. Each noun names an item/role they received. Note: ابا here is an archaic/uncertain noun in this context (not the usual sense 'refuse'), while یاره, تخت, افسر are nouns 'attendant/companion', 'throne/robe', and 'helmet' respectively; شدند is the plural past of شدن 'to become/turn into' or 'to be made'.
segments.s172-g45: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one' / 'someone'. طوس را uses را as the definite-object/topic marker attached to the recipient Tūs (classical word order allows the recipient before the verb and marked with را). داد is the past tense 'gave'. زان تخت عاج is a noun phrase 'that ivory throne' introduced by زان ('of/ from that' or 'that'), with تخت 'throne' and عاج 'ivory' as its modifier.
segments.s172-g46: همان is a demonstrative 'the same / that very', linking back to previously mentioned goods. It scopes over a coordinated list: یاره 'attendant/companion', و طوق 'and a necklace/collar', و منشور چاچ 'and the inscribed tablet/charter of Chach' — منشور چاچ is a genitive/attributive unit 'the charter/tablet of Chach' with the possessor following the noun (typical of Persian noun-noun constructions).
segments.s172-g47: ورا گفت is 'he said to him': ورا = و + را (to him/thither) placed before the verb for emphasis, and گفت is the speech verb 'said' (the provided sense list does not show the simple 'say' sense, so that is noted). هر کس که تاب آورد is a relative/conditional clause 'whoever endures / who can bear it': هر کس 'whoever', که introducing the clause, تاب آورد a compound expression where تاب 'endurance' + آورد (an auxiliary/verb) yields 'to endure' (classical idiom).
segments.s172-g48: وگر is a conditional 'or if / but if'. نام افراسیاب is a noun phrase 'the name/reputation of Afrasiab' (نام = name/reputation, plus the proper name افراسیاب). آورد is the verb 'brings / utters / mentions' (classically used in conditional constructions to mean 'brings/produces/mentions'). The whole clause offers an alternative condition: 'or if he brings (up) Afrasiab's name.'
segments.s172-g49: همانگه is an adverb meaning 'at that moment'. سرش را is the direct object: سر 'head' + enclitic ش 'his', followed by the object marker را. ز تن is a prepositional phrase 'from (his) body' (ز = from, تن = body). دور کن is an imperative construction: دور (here 'away/removed') + کن (imperative 'do'), together idiomatically 'separate' or 'cut off'. Word order places the object before the verb, typical of Persian.
segments.s172-g50: ازو 'from him' marks source. کرگسان را is the (plural) object 'the vultures' with the object marker را; the following phrase indicates what to make of him. یکی سور کن is an imperative: یکی 'one' + سور 'feast' + کن 'make' — 'make a single feast (of him)'. The construction means 'set him before the vultures / make him a feast for the vultures.'
segments.s172-g51: کسی here functions as an indefinite 'whoever' / 'someone'. کاو in classical Persian often functions as an archaic relative ('که او' = 'who/that he'), so it links کسی to the verb that follows (this use is not one of the verbal senses listed for کاو). خرد is the object 'wisdom' and جوید is the present/simple 3rd-person verb 'seeks' (classical present used for general statements). و ایمنی links a second object 'and safety' (ایمنی = security).
segments.s172-g52: نیازد is a form of نیازیدن used here in the sense 'to supplicate/pray' (third-person). سوی is a preposition 'toward/to' marking the target of the supplication. کیش آهرمنی is a noun phrase 'the creed/sect of Ahriman' (آهرمنی adjectival, 'of Ahriman'); together they indicate the object/recipient of the praying.
segments.s172-g53: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when' (or 'if'). فرزند is 'child/descendant'. باید is the modal 'must/ought' expressing obligation; که introduces the subordinate clause. داری is the present 2nd-person 'you have/hold'. به ناز is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in/with indulgence' (به + ناز), describing the manner in which one should keep the child.
segments.s172-g54: This is a sequence of adjectival/prepositional phrases describing the desired state: ز رنج 'from suffering' (ز = from) + ایمن 'safe' (predicate adjective), then از خواسته 'from desires/wants' and بی‌نیاز 'without need/self-sufficient'. The prepositions ز/از mark what the child should be protected from; the adjectives follow in classical order.
segments.s172-g55: تو is the vocative/imperative 'you'. درویش را is the object 'the dervish/poor person' marked by را. رنج is 'suffering' (object of the verb). منمای is a negative imperative ('do not cause / do not show / do not give'); in classical Persian منمای functions like 'مکن' (do not). هیچ intensifies the negation: 'not at all.' The whole segment is an imperative prohibition: 'Do not trouble the poor at all.'
segments.s172-g56: همی is a classical particle marking continuous or habitual action (often past-habitual in epic style: 'would/used to'). داد here is the past stem 'gave'. و 'and' links clauses. بر + داد دادن is a compound showing additional giving/Bestowal (بر meaning 'over/again/on'); دادن is the infinitive 'to give' used as a verbal noun/infinitive complement. بسیچ is an adverb/adjective 'much/many' modifying دادن. Overall the clause describes habitual liberal giving: 'he used to give and to give much.'
